input.prompt:
I just upgraded my .NET MAUI app from .NET 9 to .NET 10. On iOS, my content
is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

data.content:

is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

input.initialPrompt:
I just upgraded my .NET MAUI app from .NET 9 to .NET 10. On iOS, my content
is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI migration issue from .NET 9 to .NET 10 regarding safe area handling on iOS. This is a known breaking change in .NET 10 MAUI.

In .NET 10, the `UseSafeArea` attached property was removed/deprecated. The new approach uses padding-based safe area insets or the new `SafeAreaRegions` property.

Let me recall what I know:

In .NET 10 (MAUI), the `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` property was replaced. The new approach is to use `Page.IgnoreSafeArea` or the safe area behavior changed. Actually, let me think more carefully.


In .NET 9, the iOS-specific safe area configuration used the `UseSafeArea` attached property through the platform-specific namespace. In .NET 10, this API was changed—the framework now uses `IgnoreSafeArea` or a different safe area handling mechanism, though I'm still clarifying the exact replacement approach. The key change in .NET 10 is that `UseSafeArea` was deprecated in favor of a new cross-platform approach using `Page.IgnoreSafeArea` or `Page.SafeAreaRegions`, and the default safe area behavior may have shifted, so I need to clarify whether pages now ignore safe areas by default or if there's a different property to control this. Wait, but the user mentioned their content is actually going behind the safe area after upgrading to .NET 10, which means the default behavior flipped—pages stopped respecting safe area automatically. So I need to reconsider: in .NET 10, they likely removed the iOS-specific `UseSafeArea` property and moved to a cross-platform model, but the migration might require explicitly setting padding or using a new property to restore safe area respect. For layouts like `Grid` and `StackLayout`, the `IgnoreSafeArea` property has been available cross-platform. The iOS-specific `UseSafeArea` on pages was the older approach, but in .NET 10 the recommended way is to use `IgnoreSafeArea` on the layout itself or apply padding that respects safe area insets. I'm trying to pin down whether the breaking change involves removing the iOS-specific property entirely and forcing developers to use the cross-platform alternative. There might be a newer cross-platform property for this. Let me think through what I actually know here—in .NET 10, the safe area handling on iOS was reworked, so `UseSafeArea` still exists but behaves differently now. The most reliable approach I can point to is using the platform-specific API in code-behind to set safe area handling, or configuring it directly in XAML with the appropriate platform namespace. I'm thinking through what likely changed in .NET 10 MAUI - the platform-specific `UseSafeArea` property was probably replaced with a more unified approach, possibly using the Page's `Padding` property or a new cross-platform property to handle safe area insets automatically. I'll structure my answer around what I'm confident about while noting any uncertainties, and I'm starting to outline how to apply safe area padding through the page layout. I should look up the actual documentation to give an accurate answer about what changed in .NET 10 MAUI's safe area handling.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0:
The focus of .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) in .NET 10 is to improve product quality. For information about what's new in each .NET MAUI in .NET 10 release, see the following release notes:

*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Release Candidate 1](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/rc1/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 7](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview7/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 6](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview6/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 5](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview5/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 4](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview4/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 3](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview3/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 2](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview2/dotnetmaui.md)
*   [.NET MAUI in .NET 10 Preview 1](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/10.0/preview/preview1/dotnetmaui.md)

In .NET 10, .NET MAUI ships as a .NET workload and multiple NuGet packages. The advantage of this approach is that it enables you to easily pin your projects to specific versions, while also enabling you to easily preview unreleased or experimental builds.

## .NET Aspire integration

.NET MAUI for .NET 10 includes a new project template that creates a .NET Aspire service defaults project for .NET MAUI. This provides a set of extension methods that connect telemetry and service discovery to your app.

To connect telemetry and service discovery to your app, modify the `CreateMauiApp` method in your `MauiProgram` class to invoke the `AddServiceDefaults` method, from the .NET Aspire service defaults project, on the `MauiAppBuilder` object:

```
builder.AddServiceDefaults();
```

The `AddServiceDefaults` method performs the following tasks:

*   Configures OpenTelemetry metrics and tracing.
*   Adds service discovery functionality.
*   Configures [HttpClient](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.http.httpclient) to work with service discovery.

Important

The .NET Aspire service defaults project is designed for sharing the _Extensions.cs_ file and its functionality. Don't include other shared functionality or models in this project. Instead, use a shared class library project for those purposes.

For more information, see [.NET Aspire service defaults](/en-us/dotnet/aspire/fundamentals/service-defaults).

## Animation

The `FadeTo`, `LayoutTo`, `RelRotateTo`, `RelScaleTo`, `RotateTo`, `RotateXTo`, `RotateYTo`, `ScaleTo`, `ScaleXTo`, `ScaleYTo`, and `TranslateTo` methods have been deprecated and replaced with the `FadeToAsync`, `LayoutToAsync`, `RelRotateToAsync`, `RelScaleToAsync`, `RotateToAsync`, `RotateXToAsync`, `RotateYToAsync`, `ScaleToAsync`, `ScaleXToAsync`, `ScaleYToAsync`, and `TranslateToAsync` methods.

For more information, see [Basic animation](../user-interface/animation/basic?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

## Controls

.NET MAUI in .NET 10 includes control enhancements and deprecations.

### Accelerator

Removed from Microsoft.Maui.Controls. Use `KeyboardAccelerator`.

### ClickGestureRecognizer

Removed. Use `TapGestureRecognizer`.

### CollectionView and CarouselView

.NET MAUI in .NET 9 included two optional handlers on iOS and Mac Catalyst that brought performance and stability improvements to [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) and [CarouselView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.carouselview). In .NET 10, these are the default handlers for [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) and [CarouselView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.carouselview).

### Compatibility.Layout

This is now removed from templates and releases. Use .NET MAUI layouts.

### Editor and Entry on Android

On Android, the [Editor](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.editor) and [Entry](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.entry) views change their native views from `AppCompatEditText` to `MauiAppCompatEditText`, which adds support for the `SelectionChanged` event.

### HybridWebView

[HybridWebView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.hybridwebview) gains an [InvokeJavaScriptAsync](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.hybridwebview.invokejavascriptasync) overload that invokes a specified JavaScript method without specifying any information about the return type. For more information, see [Invoke JavaScript methods that don't return a value](../user-interface/controls/hybridwebview?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true#invoke-javascript-methods-that-dont-return-a-value).

By default, any exceptions that are thrown by your JavaScript code will be sent to .NET, where they're re-thrown as .NET exceptions.

In addition, you can customize initialization and access platform web views to tweak settings when the native view is ready (for example, use `RunAfterInitialize` on Windows or adjust `WKWebView` configuration on iOS/Mac Catalyst). For details, see [Customize initialization and access platform web views](../user-interface/controls/hybridwebview?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true#customize-initialization-and-access-platform-web-views).

#### New Events

Adds initialization events to `HybridWebView` following the same pattern as `BlazorWebView`, enabling platform-specific customization before and after initialization.

*   `WebViewInitializing` - Fired before `WebView` creation, allows configuration of platform-specific initialization parameters
*   `WebViewInitialized` - Fired after `WebView` creation, provides access to the native `WebView` instance and settings

### Intercept Web Requests

You can now intercept and respond to web requests made from `BlazorWebView` and `HybridWebView`. This allows for scenarios such as modifying headers, redirecting requests, or supplying local responses.

```
webView.WebResourceRequested += (s, e) =>
{
    if (e.Uri.ToString().Contains("api/secure"))
    {
        e.Handled = true;
        e.SetResponse(200, "OK", "application/json", GetCustomStream());
    }
};
```

Another example from XAML:

```
<HybridWebView WebResourceRequested="HybridWebView_WebResourceRequested" />
```

```
private void HybridWebView_WebResourceRequested(object sender, HybridWebViewWebResourceRequestedEventArgs e)
{
  // NOTES:
  // * This method MUST be synchronous, as it is called from the WebView's thread.
  // * This method MUST return a response (even if it is not yet complete), otherwise the 
  //   WebView may freeze or return a error response.
  // * The response must be set using the SetResponse method of the event args.

  // Only handle requests for the specific image URL
  if (!e.Uri.ToString().Contains("sample-image.png"))
    return;

  // Prevent the default behavior of the web view
  e.Handled = true;

  // Return the stream or task of stream that contains the content
  // NOTE: the method is NOT awaited, the WebView will continue to load the content
  e.SetResponse(200, "OK", "image/png", GetStreamAsync());
  }
```

### ListView

[ListView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.listview) has been deprecated, along with [EntryCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.entrycell), [ImageCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.imagecell), [SwitchCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.switchcell), [TextCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.textcell), and [ViewCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.viewcell). Instead, [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) should be used.

Note

[Cell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.cell) hasn't been deprecated because it's currently used for source generation. However, it should be considered deprecated.

### Page

The `IsBusy` property is marked obsolete. In .NET 10 we recommend using [ActivityIndicator](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.activityindicator) (or a similar UX) for page or view-specific busy states and avoiding long-lived global busy indicators. For guidance, see [ContentPage](../user-interface/pages/contentpage?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

### Picker

Programmatically control picker state with new Open/Close API.

In addition, [DatePicker](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker) and [TimePicker](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.timepicker) support nullable selection in .NET 10. This enables clearing values and binding to nullable types:

*   [Date](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.date#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-date) is now [DateTime](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.datetime); [MinimumDate](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.minimumdate#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-minimumdate) and [MaximumDate](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.maximumdate#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-maximumdate) are also nullable.
*   [Time](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.timepicker.time#microsoft-maui-controls-timepicker-time) is now [TimeSpan](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.timespan).

For examples and platform notes, see [DatePicker](../user-interface/controls/datepicker?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true) and [TimePicker](../user-interface/controls/timepicker?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

## RefreshView

Added `IsRefreshEnabled` property to be distinct from `IsEnabled` and make the behavior consistent across platforms.

```
<RefreshView IsRefreshEnabled="false">
    <!-- Login form remains usable -->
    <StackLayout>
        <Entry Placeholder="Username" />
        <Entry Placeholder="Password" />
        <Button Text="Login" />
    </StackLayout>
</RefreshView>
```

### SearchBar

[SearchBar](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.contr

<note>Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of 10000 to get more content.</note>

## .NET Aspire integration

.NET MAUI for .NET 10 includes a new project template that creates a .NET Aspire service defaults project for .NET MAUI. This provides a set of extension methods that connect telemetry and service discovery to your app.

To connect telemetry and service discovery to your app, modify the `CreateMauiApp` method in your `MauiProgram` class to invoke the `AddServiceDefaults` method, from the .NET Aspire service defaults project, on the `MauiAppBuilder` object:

```
builder.AddServiceDefaults();
```

The `AddServiceDefaults` method performs the following tasks:

*   Configures OpenTelemetry metrics and tracing.
*   Adds service discovery functionality.
*   Configures [HttpClient](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.http.httpclient) to work with service discovery.

Important

The .NET Aspire service defaults project is designed for sharing the _Extensions.cs_ file and its functionality. Don't include other shared functionality or models in this project. Instead, use a shared class library project for those purposes.

For more information, see [.NET Aspire service defaults](/en-us/dotnet/aspire/fundamentals/service-defaults).

## Animation

The `FadeTo`, `LayoutTo`, `RelRotateTo`, `RelScaleTo`, `RotateTo`, `RotateXTo`, `RotateYTo`, `ScaleTo`, `ScaleXTo`, `ScaleYTo`, and `TranslateTo` methods have been deprecated and replaced with the `FadeToAsync`, `LayoutToAsync`, `RelRotateToAsync`, `RelScaleToAsync`, `RotateToAsync`, `RotateXToAsync`, `RotateYToAsync`, `ScaleToAsync`, `ScaleXToAsync`, `ScaleYToAsync`, and `TranslateToAsync` methods.

For more information, see [Basic animation](../user-interface/animation/basic?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

## Controls

.NET MAUI in .NET 10 includes control enhancements and deprecations.

### Accelerator

Removed from Microsoft.Maui.Controls. Use `KeyboardAccelerator`.

### ClickGestureRecognizer

Removed. Use `TapGestureRecognizer`.

### CollectionView and CarouselView

.NET MAUI in .NET 9 included two optional handlers on iOS and Mac Catalyst that brought performance and stability improvements to [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) and [CarouselView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.carouselview). In .NET 10, these are the default handlers for [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) and [CarouselView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.carouselview).

### Compatibility.Layout

This is now removed from templates and releases. Use .NET MAUI layouts.

### Editor and Entry on Android

On Android, the [Editor](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.editor) and [Entry](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.entry) views change their native views from `AppCompatEditText` to `MauiAppCompatEditText`, which adds support for the `SelectionChanged` event.

### HybridWebView

[HybridWebView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.hybridwebview) gains an [InvokeJavaScriptAsync](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.hybridwebview.invokejavascriptasync) overload that invokes a specified JavaScript method without specifying any information about the return type. For more information, see [Invoke JavaScript methods that don't return a value](../user-interface/controls/hybridwebview?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true#invoke-javascript-methods-that-dont-return-a-value).

By default, any exceptions that are thrown by your JavaScript code will be sent to .NET, where they're re-thrown as .NET exceptions.

In addition, you can customize initialization and access platform web views to tweak settings when the native view is ready (for example, use `RunAfterInitialize` on Windows or adjust `WKWebView` configuration on iOS/Mac Catalyst). For details, see [Customize initialization and access platform web views](../user-interface/controls/hybridwebview?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true#customize-initialization-and-access-platform-web-views).

#### New Events

Adds initialization events to `HybridWebView` following the same pattern as `BlazorWebView`, enabling platform-specific customization before and after initialization.

*   `WebViewInitializing` - Fired before `WebView` creation, allows configuration of platform-specific initialization parameters
*   `WebViewInitialized` - Fired after `WebView` creation, provides access to the native `WebView` instance and settings

### Intercept Web Requests

You can now intercept and respond to web requests made from `BlazorWebView` and `HybridWebView`. This allows for scenarios such as modifying headers, redirecting requests, or supplying local responses.

```
webView.WebResourceRequested += (s, e) =>
{
    if (e.Uri.ToString().Contains("api/secure"))
    {
        e.Handled = true;
        e.SetResponse(200, "OK", "application/json", GetCustomStream());
    }
};
```

Another example from XAML:

```
<HybridWebView WebResourceRequested="HybridWebView_WebResourceRequested" />
```

```
private void HybridWebView_WebResourceRequested(object sender, HybridWebViewWebResourceRequestedEventArgs e)
{
  // NOTES:
  // * This method MUST be synchronous, as it is called from the WebView's thread.
  // * This method MUST return a response (even if it is not yet complete), otherwise the 
  //   WebView may freeze or return a error response.
  // * The response must be set using the SetResponse method of the event args.

  // Only handle requests for the specific image URL
  if (!e.Uri.ToString().Contains("sample-image.png"))
    return;

  // Prevent the default behavior of the web view
  e.Handled = true;

  // Return the stream or task of stream that contains the content
  // NOTE: the method is NOT awaited, the WebView will continue to load the content
  e.SetResponse(200, "OK", "image/png", GetStreamAsync());
  }
```

### ListView

[ListView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.listview) has been deprecated, along with [EntryCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.entrycell), [ImageCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.imagecell), [SwitchCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.switchcell), [TextCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.textcell), and [ViewCell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.viewcell). Instead, [CollectionView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.collectionview) should be used.

Note

[Cell](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.cell) hasn't been deprecated because it's currently used for source generation. However, it should be considered deprecated.

### Page

The `IsBusy` property is marked obsolete. In .NET 10 we recommend using [ActivityIndicator](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.activityindicator) (or a similar UX) for page or view-specific busy states and avoiding long-lived global busy indicators. For guidance, see [ContentPage](../user-interface/pages/contentpage?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

### Picker

Programmatically control picker state with new Open/Close API.

In addition, [DatePicker](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker) and [TimePicker](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.timepicker) support nullable selection in .NET 10. This enables clearing values and binding to nullable types:

*   [Date](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.date#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-date) is now [DateTime](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.datetime); [MinimumDate](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.minimumdate#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-minimumdate) and [MaximumDate](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.datepicker.maximumdate#microsoft-maui-controls-datepicker-maximumdate) are also nullable.
*   [Time](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.timepicker.time#microsoft-maui-controls-timepicker-time) is now [TimeSpan](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.timespan).

For examples and platform notes, see [DatePicker](../user-interface/controls/datepicker?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true) and [TimePicker](../user-interface/controls/timepicker?view=net-maui-10.0&preserve-view=true).

## RefreshView

Added `IsRefreshEnabled` property to be distinct from `IsEnabled` and make the behavior consistent across platforms.

```
<RefreshView IsRefreshEnabled="false">
    <!-- Login form remains usable -->
    <StackLayout>
        <Entry Placeholder="Username" />
        <Entry Placeholder="Password" />
        <Button Text="Login" />
    </StackLayout>
</RefreshView>
```

### SearchBar

[SearchBar](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.contr

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/ios/platform-specifics/page-safe-area-layout?view=net-maui-10.0:
By default, .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) apps automatically position page content on an area of the screen that is safe for all devices. This is known as the safe area layout guide, and ensures that content isn't clipped by rounded device corners, the home indicator, or the sensor housing on some iPhone models.

Important

In .NET 10, use the [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.safeareaedges#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-safeareaedges) property (also available on [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout), [ScrollView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.scrollview), [ContentView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentview), and [Border](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.border)) to precisely control safe area behavior. The iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` remains for existing apps, but new code should prefer `SafeAreaEdges`.

## Modern safe area guidance (.NET 10+)

### Choose the safe area behavior with SafeAreaEdges

`SafeAreaEdges` accepts the following values from [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.safeareaedges): `Default`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `All`.

*   Keep content within all safe areas (avoid bars, notch, and keyboard):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
            <!-- content stays within safe area -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Draw edge-to-edge (no safe area padding):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="None">
            <!-- content can extend behind bars and notch -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of the keyboard (soft input), but allow under top/bottom bars/notch:
    
    ```
    <ScrollView SafeAreaEdges="SoftInput">
            <!-- content can go under bars/notch but avoids keyboard overlap -->
    </ScrollView>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of top/bottom bars and notch, but allow under the keyboard:
    
    ```
    <Grid SafeAreaEdges="Container">
            <!-- content avoids bars/notch; may extend under keyboard -->
    </Grid>
    ```
    

The default value is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`) unless overridden by platform conventions.

You can set `SafeAreaEdges` in C# as well:

```
using Microsoft.Maui;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls;

// On a page
this.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.All;

// On a layout
myGrid.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.Container;

// Keyboard-aware
myScrollView.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.SoftInput;
```

### Reading the current safe area insets

To read the current safe area insets on a page at runtime, use the iOS-specific configuration API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

var insets = On<iOS>().SafeAreaInsets(); // Microsoft.Maui.Thickness
```

Tip

Safe area insets can change at runtime (for example, rotation or status bar changes). Update layout when insets change.

For more information, see:

*   [Safe area layout](../../user-interface/safe-area?view=net-maui-10.0)
*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```
<ContentPage ...
             xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
    <StackLayout>
        ...
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

On<iOS>().SetUseSafeArea(true);
```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

The [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout) class defines a [IgnoreSafeArea](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout.ignoresafearea#microsoft-maui-controls-layout-ignoresafearea) property that ensures that content is positioned on an area of the screen that is safe for all iOS devices. This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

```
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

data.content:
In .NET 10, `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` is superseded by the new **cross-platform** `SafeAreaEdges` property. The default is now edge-to-edge (content extends behind bars/notch), which is why your content shifted.

Replace:

```xml
<!-- .NET 9 (old) -->
<ContentPage xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
```

With:

```xml
<!-- .NET 10 (new) -->
<ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
    <!-- content stays within safe area on all platforms -->
</ContentPage>
```

**`SafeAreaEdges` values:**
| Value | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `All` | Avoids bars, notch, **and** keyboard |
| `Container` | Avoids bars/notch, but allows under keyboard |
| `SoftInput` | Allows under bars/notch, avoids keyboard |
| `None` | Full edge-to-edge (no safe area padding) |

It also works on `Grid`, `ScrollView`, `ContentView`, and `Border` for more granular control. No iOS-specific namespace needed.
data.reasoningText:
I now have the official documentation. The key information is:

In .NET 10, the new cross-platform `SafeAreaEdges` property replaces the iOS-specific `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"`. The old property still works but the new approach is preferred.

The default value of `SafeAreaEdges` is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`). This explains why the user's content is going behind the status bar and notch — the default changed to edge-to-edge.

The fix is to use `SafeAreaEdges="All"` on the ContentPage.
